summary: Gruber responds to Peter-Paul Koch's argument that iPhone developers should bypass the App Store by writing web apps instead. While acknowledging that web apps are under-utilized and offer cross-platform benefits, Gruber argues that Koch greatly overstates what's possible with web apps in terms of user experience. Native Cocoa Touch apps are faster, more capable, and easier to write than their web equivalents. Gruber also disputes Koch's claim that the App Store was a rushed afterthought, arguing Apple always planned a native SDK. He concludes that while web apps may eventually close the gap, it's not credible to claim they're already a serious alternative to native apps.
key_points: iPhone web apps can be good but cannot match the speed, grace, or capability of native Cocoa Touch apps — as demonstrated by comparing native Twitter clients to the web-based Hahlo
Web apps have hard technical limitations like no access to the camera or image library, making certain app categories impossible
Apple's own behavior proves the point: virtually all of Apple's iPhone apps use Cocoa Touch, not web technologies
The 'sweet solution' of web-only development was likely a stopgap while the native SDK was being finalized, not a failed long-term strategy
Future hardware improvements and expanded web APIs could eventually close the gap, but that hypothetical future hasn't arrived yet
money_quotes: The argument that you can make iPhone web apps that are 'good enough' misses the entire point of iPhone apps — the entire point of the iPhone itself, even.
'Good enough' is not good enough on the iPhone.
Successful iPhone developers don't just want to write software that works on the iPhone. They want to write software for the iPhone that's just as good as Apple's.
Not only are native iPhone apps faster and more capable than their web-app equivalents, but they're easier to write.
There is no way anyone could write an iPhone web app that works as well or feels as good as any of the top native iPhone Twitter clients.
It isn't credible to argue that they already are.
key_insight: The iPhone's defining quality is the premium user experience enabled by native Cocoa Touch development, and arguing that web apps are an equivalent alternative fundamentally misunderstands what makes the platform compelling.
